coredata.dc:title: Preparation of carbon dots-hematite quantum dots-loaded hydroxypropyl cellulose-chitosan nanocomposites for drug delivery, sunlight catalytic and antimicrobial application
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology B: Biology
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Yanuo
Cheng, Haixiang
Wang, Weina
Jin, Zhe
Liu, Qi
Yang, Huayun
Cao, Yong
Li, Weidong
Fakhri, Ali
Gupta, Vinod Kumar
coredata.dc:description: In this project, we studied the thermal and chemical method for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs)/Hematite (α-Fe2O3) quantum dots and the preparation of hydroxypropyl cellulose cross-linked chitosan (HPCCS) and ulvan (UN) was performed by chemical method. Carbon dots/α-Fe2O3 quantum dots with size distribution of 3–5 nm were completely encapsulated in the HPCCS/UN NPs to obtain composites, which indicated unique characteristics with respect to antimicrobial, pH-responsive and optical properties. The CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN nanocomposites exhibited a single-excitation (440 nm), dual-emission fluorescence property (505 nm and 628 nm for green and red light from CDs-HQDs and HPCCS/UN NPs). The nanocomposites played as a pH-responsive drug delivery process to release ulvan at a fast rate in pH 7.4 buffer solution but at a slow rate in low pH solutions. The CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN nanocomposites gained the highest photocatalytic activity for degrading 4-chlorophenol (4-CPh) as a pollutant (>98% during 70 min under sunlight irradiation). Moreover, the nanocomposites indicated great inhibitory influences towards bacterial and fungal.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dot
Biopolymer
Hematite
Catalytic
Antimicrobial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation and Characterization of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs

Y201224448 ), and Educational Science Planning Project of Zhejiang Province (no. 2021SCG031 ), and Hangzhou Science and Technology Development Foundation of China (no. 20190101A02 ), and Competitive Projects in Science and Technology Programmes of Quzhou City (no. 2020K08 ), and Science Foundation of Qianjiang College (no. 2021QJXS09 & 2021QJJL06 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) The particle size and zeta potential of HPCCS/UN NPs prepared at different ratios, (B) Zeta potential, and size distribution (C), FESEM image, (D) FTIR spectra of HPCCS/UN NPs 1:1.

(A) The mean particle size and zeta potential of CDs-HQDs, (B) the excitation and emission spectra of CDs-HQDs, (C) photoluminescence spectra of the CDs-HQDs excited at different wavelength, (D) photoluminescence spectra of the CDs-HQDs excited at different pH.
(A, B, C) fluorescence spectra of CDs-HQDs, HPCCS/UN NPs, crosslinking-HPCCS/UN NPs excited at 380 and 550 nm, and CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs excited at 440 nm, (D) FESEM images of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs.

(A) The release profiles of UN from CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN in pH 2.0, 6.0 and 7.4, (B) 20-h release of CDs-HQDs from CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN and the emission intensity at 510 nm to 630 nm in pH 2.0, 6.0 and 7.4, (C) Cell viability of A549 cells cultured with different concentrations of samples.
The photo-degradation of 4-chlorophenol under sun light irradiation.
Antibacterial and antifungal activities against E. coli, S. aureus, C. albicans, and A. niger.

head.ce:title: Preparation of carbon dots-hematite quantum dots-loaded hydroxypropyl cellulose-chitosan nanocomposites for drug delivery, sunlight catalytic and antimicrobial application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Polytechnic Institute of Qianjiang College, Hangzhou Normal University, Hangzhou, Zhejiang 310018, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Polytechnic Institute of Qianjiang College, Hangzhou Normal University, Hangzhou Zhejiang, 310018, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Nano Smart Science Institute (NSSI), Tehran, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: f
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biological Sciences, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Biological Sciences, Faculty of Science, King Abdulaziz University, Jeddah, Saudi Arabia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this project, we studied the thermal and chemical method for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs)/Hematite (α-Fe2O3) quantum dots and the preparation of hydroxypropyl cellulose cross-linked chitosan (HPCCS) and ulvan (UN) was performed by chemical method. Carbon dots/α-Fe2O3 quantum dots with size distribution of 3–5 nm were completely encapsulated in the HPCCS/UN NPs to obtain composites, which indicated unique characteristics with respect to antimicrobial, pH-responsive and optical properties. The CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN nanocomposites exhibited a single-excitation (440 nm), dual-emission fluorescence property (505 nm and 628 nm for green and red light from CDs-HQDs and HPCCS/UN NPs). The nanocomposites played as a pH-responsive drug delivery process to release ulvan at a fast rate in pH 7.4 buffer solution but at a slow rate in low pH solutions. The CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN nanocomposites gained the highest photocatalytic activity for degrading 4-chlorophenol (4-CPh) as a pollutant (>98% during 70 min under sunlight irradiation). Moreover, the nanocomposites indicated great inhibitory influences towards bacterial and fungal.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new luminescent carbon-based nanomaterials with the substantial optical properties and broad range of applications [1]. Hematite (α-Fe2O3) quantum dots are the most promising materials due to its abundance, nontoxicity, stability, and optical properties [2]. CDs and HQDs have lower cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility, making it very suitable for environmental and biomedical applications such as metal pollution detection [3,4], photocatalytic process [5,6], bioimaging [7,8], cancer treatment [9], and drug delivery [10]. CDs and HQDs were used to modification with ligands on surface or incorporated in carriers for drug delivery. Hereupon, the combination of CDs and HQDs with biological macromolecules was performed to increase functional properties for biomedical imaging and therapeutics properties [11,12].

Recently, the previous studies showed that the low antimicrobial activity of CDs and HQDs [13]. Accordingly, CDs and HQDs have been modified or combined with poly-γ-glutamic acid [14], cellulose [15], polysaccharide hydrogel [16] and chitosan [17], to improve their antibacterial performance. Therefore, chitosan (CS) and hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC) were the excellent candidates to modification antibacterial process for biomedical and pharmaceutical applications. Hydroxypropyl cellulose (HPC) is a cellulose material with excellent water solubility and used in the biomedicine filed due to their substantial biocompatibility, biodegradability, antimicrobial activities [18,19]. Ulvan is a sulfated polysaccharide extracted from green algae after hydrolysis and applied as a biomaterial in nanomedicine and drug delivery due to biological, pharmacological and tissue repairing activities [20].

This project aims to preparation of CDs-HQDs and HPCCS/UN NPs combined nanocomposites to decrease their hydrophilicity. The nanocomposites prepared by incorporating CDs-HQDs in HPCCS/UN NPs and showed the excellent optical property of dual-fluorescence emission. The photocatalytic properties of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS was investigated for degradation process under sunlight irradiation. Furthermore, the antibacterial activity of the nanocomposites were also investigated.
Materials and reagents were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd. (USA).

The 30 mL of ulvan aqueous solution (5 mg/mL) was depolymerized with 0.1 M of H2O2 under stirring at 70 °C for 2 h. The reaction was performed by addition of 0.2 M of Na₂CO₃. The ulvan were then purified by dialysis tube.

The 30 mL of HPC solution (2% concentration) was prepared, while the 30 mL of CS solution (2% concentration) was obtained using acetic acid solution 1%. These solutions were mixed with equal ratio. Then, 10 mL of epichlorohydrin/hydrochloric acid (1:1) were added and stirring was continued for 40 min. The solution was dispensed, sealed and placed in a thermostatic water bath at 60 °C for 60 min. After the crosslinking process, the product was dried in vacuum oven at 45 °C for 5 h.

The aqueous solution of ulvan (2 mg/mL) was slowly added to the aqueous solution of HPC (2 mg/mL). The procured mixture was stirred at 27 °C under vigorous stirring to collect the dispersion of HPCCS/ulvan NPs.

The FeCl3 (0.3 g) was dissolved in 10 mL of oleic acid in a Teflon autoclave under stirring. Isopropanol (35 mL) was then added under constant stirring. The autoclave was transferred into a microwave autoclave reactor. The temperature was maintained at 180 °C in 40 min. After cooling down, the α-Fe2O3 quantum dots were washed with absolute ethanol/acetone and dried in vacuum at 55 °C for 7 h.

The L-alanine (0.3 g) was dissolved in 10mL deionized water under stirring and the 20 mL of citric acid was added. The mixing process was conducted at 60 °C for 40 min. After ultrasonication for 30min, the mixture was sealed for 15min in a microwave oven. The product was cooled and dissolved in 10mL deionized water under stirring. Finally, it was filtered to remove the impurity. The CDs was extracted by ethyl acetate and then kept in a dark under 5 °C.

The glyoxal (C2H2O2) (40 μL) and HPCCS (5 mg/mL) solutions were mixed under vigorous stirring. The CDs-HQDs (5 mg/mL) were dispersed in crosslinking HPCCS solution and then the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS mixture was added to the ulvan solution to obtain loading composites. The procured mixture was stirred at 27 °C under vigorous stirring to collect the dispersion of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/ulvan NPs. The product was dried in vacuum oven at 45 °C for 5 h.

The sizes and zeta potentials of particles were measured using a Malvern Zetasizer. The Fourier transform infrared spectra were conducted on a Perkin-Elmer Spectrum FT-IR spectrometer. The morphological data were studied by field emission scanning electron microscopy (Mira 3 TESCAN). The fluorescence emission spectra were measured using EnSpire 2300 Multilabel Plate Reader (Perkin Elmer). The UV–visible spectrophotometer was performed using UV– visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, UV-1900).

Ulvan and CDs-HQDs release from composites were distinguished in different buffer solutions at 37 °C. CDs-HQDs was determined by measuring the fluorescence intensity by using a Perkin Elmer EnSpire 2300 multimode plate reader. Ulvan release from composites was investigated by the BaCl2 gelatin method [19].

A549 Human lung carcinoma cell lines were seeded into 96-well plates at 1 × 104 cells/well and incubated in culture medium for 24 h. After washing the cells with PBS, the culture was replaced with culture containing different amounts of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN, and cultured for overnight. Then, 20 μL MTT (5 mg/mL in PBS) was added to each well and the cells were incubated for 5 h at 37 °C. After dissolution of the MTT formazan precipitate with DMSO, the absorbance values were measured using a PerkinElmer plate reader.

20 mg of the prepared catalyst was added to 10 mg/L aqueous solutions of 4-chlorophenol (4-CPh). The reaction was conducted in a cylindrical photo reactor, and the solution was stirred in the dark condition for 1 h to achieve a reaction equilibrium. The photocatalytic test was conducted under outside solar light with on sunny days. Then, the residual concentration of 4-CPh was determined at 230 nm by using a UV–visible spectrophotometer. Similar test was conducted in the absence of a catalyst.

The antibacterial efficiency of the prepared nanomaterials was studied against Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli by using Mueller-Hinton agar (MHA) plates. Each plate contains 30 μL of 106 CFU of S. aureus and E. coli suspensions and the prepared nano-suspension (10, 20, and 50 μg per well). The test plates were incubated for overnight at 37 °C. To study the activity of the prepared nanomaterials on Aspergillus niger and Candida albicans growth were studied by well diffusion method. Petri plates were used to prepare 20 mL of MHA. The 10, 20, and 50 μg of the prepared nano-suspension were loaded on the wells. These plates were incubated at 37 °C for overnight. The zone of inhibition was noticed in millimeters (mm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and Characterization of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs
ce:section.ce:para: The average particle size and zeta potential were determined for different HPCCS-to-UN weight ratios with a DLS zetasizer. Fig. 1A shows that the sizes of HPCCS/UN NPs ranged 105–182 nm and the zeta potential enhanced as the HPCCS/UN weight ratio enhanced. The enhanced zeta potential was indicated the excess positively charged of HPCCS or negatively charged UN on the NPs surface and complex formation by electrostatic interactions. The zeta potential and mean particle size of the HPCCS/UN (1:1) NPs were 19.22 ± 0.6 mV and 152.8 ± 1.5 nm, indicating that the NPs surface were covered with HPCS (Fig. 1B). FESEM images depicts that HPCCS/UN (1:1) NPs were prepared as spherical particles with smooth surface (Fig. 1C). Fig. 1D depicts the FTIR spectra of UN, and HPCCS/UN (1:1) NPs. The FTIR spectrum of ulvan indicated SO and COS stretching band in the range of 1050–1250 cm−1 and 680–780 cm−1, which was indicated to the sulfate ester groups in ulvan. The FTIR spectra depicts the absorption bands at 2965 cm−1, 1460 cm−1 and 1315 cm−1 of hydroxypropyl. The broad absorption peaks observed at 3400 and 1070 cm−1 are defined to OH, NH and C–O–C stretching from saccharide ring of HPCCS. The electrostatic interactions was conducted between the oppositely charged ulvan and HPCCS [19,20]. The FTIR spectra of HPCS/UN NPs, the characteristic CO, sulfated ester and NH3 bands shifted from 1730, 1250 and 3400 cm−1 to 1739, 1254 and 3419 cm−1, respectively, confirming the interactions between HPCCS and UN.

The FESEM analysis of CDs-HQDs, and indicates that well-distributed and uniform in size (figure not shown). Fig. 2A shows that the DLS curve of CDs-HQDs with average size less than 8 nm and the zeta potential is −5.0 mV. Fig. 2B depicts the optical properties including excitation and emission spectra of the CDs-HQDs. The excitation and emission spectra of the CDs-HQDs depicts an excitation peak at 348 nm and an emission peak at 456 nm due to the presence of dots = N, dots = O and dots = C surface functional groups. Fig. 2C depicts the photoluminescence emission spectra of the CDs-HQDs excited at different wavelength. The CDs-HQDs depicts an excitation-emission properties and the emission shifted to higher wavelengths with enhancing the excitation wavelength from 300 nm to 460 nm. The maximum emission was observed at 457 nm under the excitation at 380 nm. The PL emission of CDs-HQDs was formed due to the quantum size effect and surface defects [21]. The pH-dependent fluorescence properties of the CDs-HQDs was studied to realize the suitable pH range for bioimaging and drug delivery. Fig. 2D depicts that CDs-HQDs has the same process in fluorescence emission spectra for various pH values. With the excitation wavelength enhances, the emission wavelength showed a red shift and was attended by reduces in fluorescence intensity. The fluorescence spectra decreased with the rise of pH values from 5.0 to 7.0, which that showed the fluorescence emission wasn't influenced by pH values. Fig. 3 depicts the fluorescence spectra of CDs-HQDs, HPCCS/UN NPs and crosslinking-HPCCS/UN NPs excited UV lights (380 nm) and visible light (550 nm). When excited at 380 nm, the emission intensity of CDs-HQDs was higher than other samples. These results presented that CDs-HQDs predominated the fluorescence emission when excited by UV lights but crosslinking-HPCCS/UN NPs predominated the emission when excited at a wavelength of 550 nm of visible light. The fluorescence properties of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs was studied. The wavelength of 440 nm was investigated to excite the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs due to the fluorescence intensity of CDs-HQDs and crosslinking-HPCCS/UN at 380 nm and 550 nm, respectively. Fig. 3C shows the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs revealed dual stronger and weaker emission from CDs-HQDs and crosslinking-HPCCS/UN by excitation in 440 nm, respectively. The zeta potential and average particle size of the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs were 25.1 ± 2.3 mV and 187.5 ± 1.2 nm (figure not shown). FESEM images shows that CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs were composed of CDs-HQDs and spherical particles (Fig. 3D).

The release profiles of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs were conducted at pH 2.0, 6.0, and 7.4 to simulate the gastric fluid, infected wound and intestinal fluid. The release of ulvan is important role for its physiological activities, due to antioxidant, anti-inflammatory and anticancer properties of ulvan [21,22,23]. Fig. 4A depicts the release rate of UN from CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs enhanced with the pH enhancing from 2.0 and 6.0 to 7.4, due to stability of the NPs at pH less than 6.5. At pH 2.0 and 6.0, the UN was released about 23.45% and 30.51% after 20 h, respectively. The result displays that ulvan is protected in CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs, and thus the rapid release was conducted in gastric fluid and control the release was conducted at the wound site. At pH 7.4, the fast release was conducted about 73.4% in 20 h, showing that the NPs can release of impressive ulvan and treat chronic inflammation of the gastrointestinal tract [69]. The different release behaviors at pH 2.0, 6.0 and 7.4 can be showed that the stable of electrostatic attractions at pH 2.0 and 6.0. As shown in Fig. 4B, the NPs indicated a higher degree of CDs-HQDs release (83.32%) at pH 7.4 for compared to pH 2.0 and 6.0, displaying that the CDs-HQDs release was pH-dependent and faster at high pH conditions. Therefore, the fluorescence properties of the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs is investigated by the pH conditions. The ratio of the emission505/628 was enhanced 2.4 to 4.3 at pH 7.4, indicating that CDs-HQDs was greatly released from NPs. The ratio was fixed in pH 2.0 and 6.0, which depicting that the fluorescence properties was stable and was not affected by the H+ ions. The outcomes indicated that the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs had the potential for pH-dependent release system.

The cytotoxic effect of the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs were conducted against A549 cells [24]. Cell viability values of CDs-HQDs, crosslinking-HPCCS/UN NPs and CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs against A549 clearly indicated that even at higher concentrations (40.66% with 60 μg/mL), the NPs have significant cytotoxicity in the cells (Fig. 4C). As can be seen, the cytotoxicity of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs was higher than other the prepared nanomaterials.

The photo-degradation properties of the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS nanoparticles was studied by the degradation of the 4-CPh under sunlight irradiations. The 4-CPh solution was reacted as photolysis in the absence of the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS nanoparticles (Fig. 5 ). The result shows the degradation of some amount of 4-CPh within 2 h irradiation time. The degradation amount of 4-CPh in the absence of catalyst are 10.5% under sunlight irradiation. After the addition of the catalyst, a reaction equilibrium was reached in 30 min to 8.23% 4-CPh degraded by using CDs-HQDs/HPCCS. The photo-degradation of 4-CPh was done 98.64% by CDs-HQDs/HPCCS nanoparticles under sunlight irradiation in 70 min. The photocatalytic amount of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS nanoparticles was higher than HPCCS, due to the substantial optical and electronical properties of carbon dots and α-Fe2O3 quantum dots [25–28]. The photocatalytic reactions follow the kinetic model (Langmuir-Hinshelwood). The rate of photochemical reactions are proportional to the surface coverage of the catalyst [29–60]. The –ln (C0/C) versus irradiation time was plotting for the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS nanoparticles, to evaluation kinetic rate from slop of this plot. The kinetic rate of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS nanoparticles, and HPCCS nanoparticles were 0.223, and 0.092 min−1, respectively.

The antibacterial efficiency of the prepared nanomaterials tested versus S. aureus and E. coli pathogens as shown in Fig. 6 . The synthesized CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs has been showed great antibacterial efficiency compared to control group. This could be related to the presence of UN and more antibacterial feature in the samples. The zone of inhibition was determined with varying concentrations of 10 μg, 20 μg and 50 μg. The zone of inhibition of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs has been indicated anti-bacterial efficacy towards S. aureus and E. coli were 14.04 mm, and 18.54 mm for 50 μg per well, respectively [61–65]. Fig. 6 has illustrated the antifungal activity of the prepared nanomaterials with a different zone. The A. niger and C. albicans were utilized to evaluation of the antifungal activity. The different prepared nanomaterials concentrations were used as 10 μg, 20 μg, and 50 μg. The antifungal activity of CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs of A. niger and C. albicans was found to 17.78 mm, and 22.11 mm for 50 μg per well, respectively. The results showed that the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN has a better antifungal efficiency [66–70].

In this work, the microwave-assisted chemical synthesis method for preparation of carbon dots and α-Fe2O3 quantum dots with blue fluorescence emission under excitation at 348 nm. The crosslinking HPCS/UN NPs was prepared with red fluorescence emission under excitation at 550 nm. The carbon dots and α-Fe2O3 quantum dots were loaded in the HPCCS/UN NPs, and indicated the dual fluorescence emission. The carbon dots and α-Fe2O3 quantum dots release is slow in pH 2.0 and 6.0, but is greatly accelerated in pH 7.4. Moreover, the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs showed stronger cytotoxicity activity than other samples. The CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs displayed an excellent photocatalytic efficiency and the kinetics of the reaction was found to the degrading of 4-chlorophenol (98.64%) under 70 min sunlight irradiation. The results showed that the CDs-HQDs/HPCCS/UN NPs has a substantial antimicrobial activity.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (no. 21675037), Science Foundation of Education Bureau of Zhejiang Province, China (no. Y201224448), and Educational Science Planning Project of Zhejiang Province (no. 2021SCG031), and Hangzhou Science and Technology Development Foundation of China (no. 20190101A02), and Competitive Projects in Science and Technology Programmes of Quzhou City (no. 2020K08), and Science Foundation of Qianjiang College (no. 2021QJXS09 & 2021QJJL06).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent trends in the use of green sources for carbon dot synthesis–a short review
Carbon Trends

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Manju Kurian, Anju Paul, Recent trends in the use of green sources for carbon dot synthesis–A short review, Carbon Trends, 3, 2021, 100032.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: α-Fe2O3 quantum dots: low-cost synthesis and photocatalytic oxygen evolution capabilities
RSC Adv.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Jian Wang, Ning Zhang, Jinzhan Su, Liejin Guo, α-Fe2O3 Quantum Dots: Low-cost Synthesis and Photocatalytic Oxygen Evolution Capabilities, RSC Adv., 6, 2016, 41060-41066.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly-dispersed Fe2O3@C electrode materials for Pb2+ removal by capacitive deionization
Carbon

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Haiying Wang, Yingjie He, Jian Luo, Highly-dispersed Fe2O3@C electrode materials for Pb2+ removal by capacitive deionization, Carbon, 153, 2019, 12-20.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Applications of carbon dots in environmental pollution control: a review
Chem. Eng. J.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Caicheng Long, Zixin Jiang, Bo Feng, Applications of carbon dots in environmental pollution control: A review, Chemical Engineering Journal, 406, 2021, 126848.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A review on exploration of Fe2O3 photocatalyst towards degradation of dyes and organic contaminants
J. Environ. Manag.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. N. C. Hitam, A. A. Jalil, A review on exploration of Fe2O3 photocatalyst towards degradation of dyes and organic contaminants, Journal of Environmental Management, 258, 2020, 110050.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Metal free, sunlight and white light based photocatalysis using carbon quantum dots from Citrus grandis: a green way to remove pollution
Sol. Energy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Venkadeshkumar Ramar, Shaheer Moothattu, Karthikeyan Balasubramanian, Metal free, sunlight and white light based photocatalysis using carbon quantum dots from Citrus grandis: A green way to remove pollution, Solar Energy, 169, 2018, 120-127.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ultra-high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their versatile application in fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting
Spectrochim. Acta A Mol. Biomol. Spectrosc.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Anzhong Tan, Guanghui Yang, Xuejuan Wan, Ultra-high quantum yield nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots and their versatile application in fluorescence sensing, bioimaging and anti-counterfeiting, Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy, 253, 2021, 119583.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Seed-mediated synthesis, properties and application of γ-Fe2O3–CdSe magnetic quantum dots
J. Solid State Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alex W. H. Lin, Chung Yen Ang, Jackie Y. Ying, Seed-mediated synthesis, properties and application of γ-Fe2O3–CdSe magnetic quantum dots, Journal of Solid State Chemistry, 184, 2011, 2150-2158.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Keerthika Devi Ramadhass, Gopu Gopalakrishnan, synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@Fe2O3/multiwall carbon nanotubes ternary nanocomposite for the simultaneous electrochemical detection of 5-fluorouracil, uric acid, and xanthine
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Muthusankar Ganesan, Keerthika Devi Ramadhass, Gopu Gopalakrishnan, Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@Fe2O3/multiwall carbon nanotubes ternary nanocomposite for the simultaneous electrochemical detection of 5-fluorouracil, uric acid, and xanthine, Journal of Molecular Liquids, 331, 2021, 115768.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Natural carbon-based quantum dots and their applications in drug delivery: a review
Biomed. Pharmacother.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Akhila Nair, Jozef T. Haponiuk, Sreeraj Gopi, Natural carbon-based quantum dots and their applications in drug delivery: A review, Biomedicine & Pharmacotherapy, 132, 2020, 110834.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chitosan/carbon quantum dot/aptamer complex as a potential anticancer drug delivery system towards the release of 5-fluorouracil
Int. J. Biol. Macromol.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Hediyeh Sepahi Zavareh, Mehrab Pourmadadi, Meisam Omidi, Chitosan/carbon quantum dot/aptamer complex as a potential anticancer drug delivery system towards the release of 5-fluorouracil, International Journal of Biological Macromolecules, 165, 2020, 1422-1430.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: One pot synthesis of carbon dots decorated carboxymethyl cellulose- hydroxyapatite nanocomposite for drug delivery, tissue engineering and Fe3+ ion sensing
Carbohydr. Polym.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chandrani Sarkar, Angshuman Ray Chowdhuri, Sumanta Kumar Sahu, One pot synthesis of carbon dots decorated carboxymethyl cellulose- hydroxyapatite nanocomposite for drug delivery, tissue engineering and Fe3+ ion sensing, Carbohydrate Polymers, 181, 2018, 710-718.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antimicrobial activity, cytotoxicity and DNA binding studies of carbon dots
